I would like to set the keystone token like that line 4:
https://review.openstack.org/#/c/159538/4/gnocchi/tests/gabbi/gabbits_gate/base.yaml
可以参考一下 [nltk 的实现](http://www.nltk.org/_modules/nltk/text.html#TextCollection)，先计算所有词的 IDF

```
>>> tuple(jieba.cut('丰田太省了'))
('丰田', '太省', '了')
>>> tuple(jieba.cut('丰田太省了',HMM=0))
('丰田', '太', '省', '了')
```

It's a 'new word' found by the HMM model because the model thinks '太省' is probably a word. So is the case of '最'. If you want to get the result only according to the dictionary, set `HMM=False`.

Somehow it would have been easier if I would have known that I need to setup a secure environment to avoid the error message. Somehow it is for me not so obvious that you need to understand first

* [Keys, Certificates and CA Certificates](https://www.rabbitmq.com/ssl.html)
* [Setup of Celery Security](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/configuration.html#security)
    
and then setup secure keys, etc.

    CELERY_SECURITY_KEY = '/etc/ssl/private/worker.key'
    CELERY_SECURITY_CERTIFICATE = '/etc/ssl/certs/worker.pem'
    CELERY_SECURITY_CERT_STORE = '/etc/ssl/certs/*.pem'
    from celery.security import setup_security
    setup_security()

to avoid the above error message. 

It would be nice to include the explanation somewhere in the documentation and if in the source DEBUG would be used at these parts (serializer.py, line 174, TRUSTED_CONTENT, etc. ) as you would do in Django core.

> Is it correct that you can't see a difference between an (incorrect) UUID4 in Faker and a valid UUID4?

That is not correct. And 15/16 times it will produce a UUID that is not version 4.

```
>>> u = uuid.UUID(int=random.getrandbits(128))
>>> u
UUID('7119ccd3-e9be-c160-8d0d-608e5f5ea285')
>>> u.version
12
```

There are a total of 8 bits that must be set manually in order for it to be properly detected as a UUIDv4.

```
>>> uuid.UUID(int=(random.getrandbits(128) | (1<<63) | (1<<78)) & (~(1<<79) & ~(1<<77) & ~(1<<76) & ~(1<<62))).version
4
>>> uuid.UUID(int=(random.getrandbits(128) | (1<<63) | (1<<78)) & (~(1<<79) & ~(1<<77) & ~(1<<76) & ~(1<<62))).version
4
>>> uuid.UUID(int=(random.getrandbits(128) | (1<<63) | (1<<78)) & (~(1<<79) & ~(1<<77) & ~(1<<76) & ~(1<<62))).version
4
>>> uuid.UUID(int=(random.getrandbits(128) | (1<<63) | (1<<78)) & (~(1<<79) & ~(1<<77) & ~(1<<76) & ~(1<<62))).version
4
>>> uuid.UUID(int=(random.getrandbits(128) | (1<<63) | (1<<78)) & (~(1<<79) & ~(1<<77) & ~(1<<76) & ~(1<<62))).version
4
```

The issue concerning the favorite-inconsistencies is actually quite simple: I didn't turn the mature-content filter off.
This wasn't necessary for the [/gallery/all API endpoint](http://www.deviantart.com/developers/http/v1/20160316/gallery_all/bdb19761e6debcc6609356d6b78f4a5d), which is used to get a users submitted deviations, but it seems to be needed to get all of a users favorites via the [/collections/{folderid} API endpoint](http://www.deviantart.com/developers/http/v1/20160316/collections/834f7f78540bc3b092f1876bbae30869).

> Would be interesting to know what the API returns.

I first use [/collections/folders](http://www.deviantart.com/developers/http/v1/20160316/collections_folders/35030a41b12db6c4edf6bf9b163e4327) to get a list of all favorite-folders, which includes the "Featured" listing, but not the one for "All". This result is then used with [/collections/{folderid}](https://www.deviantart.com/developers/http/v1/20160316/collections/834f7f78540bc3b092f1876bbae30869) to get a list of all deviation-objects. You can even [try this out yourself](http://www.deviantart.com/developers/console/collections/collections_folders/35030a41b12db6c4edf6bf9b163e4327?username=hart-worx&calculate_size=false&ext_preload=false&offset=&limit=&da_version=&grant_type=client_credentials&mature_content=false&endpoint=%2Fcollections%2Ffolders).
There doesn't seem to be any way to get the "All" listing via API. For most users this is probably just the total of all the collection-listings, but http://rick35mm.deviantart.com/favourites/?catpath=/ only has 4 items, whereas there are 44 in the "Featured" listing.

Another note: ../favourites/ and ../favourites/?catpath=/ both get currently treated as one and the same and result in the "Featured" listing being downloaded.

The function definition is:

```python
"""Estimate the Orientation and Script of the image.

Returns:
    `dict` or `None` if image was not successfully processed. dict contains:
        - orientation: Orientation ids [0..3] map to [0, 270, 180, 90] degree orientations of the
          page respectively, where the values refer to the amount of clockwise
          rotation to be applied to the page for the text to be upright and readable.
        - oconfidence: Orientation confidence.
        - script: Index of the script with the highest score for this orientation.
        - sconfidence: script confidence.
"""
```

Ok, done it. First, a webhook that responds to **chat messages** only. Note that you can't use the raw request data right away, have to parse it yourself.

```python
import sys
import json
from flask import Flask, request, Response
import telepot

TOKEN = sys.argv[1]
PORT = int(sys.argv[2])
URL = sys.argv[3]

app = Flask(__name__)
bot = telepot.Bot(TOKEN)

@app.route('/abc', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def pass_update():
    msg = json.loads(request.data)['message']

    r = {'method': 'sendMessage',
         'chat_id': msg['chat']['id'],
         'text': 'Quick response'}

    return Response(json.dumps(telepot._rectify(r)), mimetype='application/json')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    bot.setWebhook(URL)
    app.run(port=PORT, debug=True)
```

Then, a webhook that responds to **inline query**.

```python
import sys
import json
from flask import Flask, request, Response
import telepot

TOKEN = sys.argv[1]
PORT = int(sys.argv[2])
URL = sys.argv[3]

app = Flask(__name__)
bot = telepot.Bot(TOKEN)

@app.route('/abc', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def pass_update():
    iq = json.loads(request.data)['inline_query']
    query_id = iq['id']

    articles = [{'type': 'article',
                    'id': 'abc', 'title': 'ABC', 'message_text': 'Good morning'}]

    r = {'method': 'answerInlineQuery',
         'inline_query_id': query_id,
         'results': articles}

    return Response(json.dumps(telepot._rectify(r)), mimetype='application/json')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    bot.setWebhook(URL)
    app.run(port=PORT, debug=True)
```

Assuming that you can't upload files with a direct response, anything you can do in that response has to be quite simple and straight-forward. With the help of `_rectify`, it is so simple and straight-forward that you may as well construct the response yourself, without going through telepot.

In short, you can't: the render `canvas` method encapsulates and manages the image and drawing surface entirely. Either something like (untested):

```python
device = ...
bkgnd = Image.new(device.mode, device.size)
bkgnd.paste(wifiIcon, (0, 0))
with canvas(device, bkgnd) as draw:
    draw.line((0, 0) + device.size, fill=128)
    draw.line((0, device.size[1], device.size[0], 0), fill=128)
```

or dont use the canvas at all, and initiate an unmanaged draw object:

```python
device = ...
im = Image.new(device.mode, device.size)
im.paste(wifiIcon, (0, 0))
draw = ImageDraw.Draw(im)
draw.line((0, 0) + device.size, fill=128)
draw.line((0, device.size[1], device.size[0], 0), fill=128)
del draw
device.display(bkgnd)
```

[All the `canvas` context _really_ does is manage creating the draw object and deleting it afterwards]

The motivation for this PR is that you may want to a) write code that can do things after initiating a streaming API request and/or b) have multiple concurrent streaming API requests going on. With the current revision of the library, you can manage it via calling the library on a thread, but there's no way to indicate to the library that the connection should be closed, and since AFAICT python doesn't allow termination of threads outside of the thread itself, there's no way to terminate the thread until the server sends something that prompts requests to hand control back to your code. Returning an object that controls this connection hands the power back to the consumer, allowing you to terminate it when you want to, rather than "next time the server has something."

> Is this by design?

Yes, this is by design. Maya always converts the parsed datetimes to UTC.
That's what allows what's listed here: [Why is this useful](https://github.com/kennethreitz/maya#-why-is-this-useful)

If you want to convert it back from a `MayaDT` instance to a timezone aware `datetime` object you can do the following:

```pycon
>>> m = maya.parse('2018-04-23T12:00:00+05:30')
>>> m.datetime()
datetime.datetime(2018, 4, 23, 6, 30, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>> m.datetime(to_timezone='Asia/Kolkata')
datetime.datetime(2018, 4, 23, 12, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Kolkata' IST+5:30:00 STD>)
```

The loaders are at https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/tree/master/src/trainers , and the main is at https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/main/train.cpp  Where are you seeing epsilon?

As far as the training nans... training nans is a perennial problem with neural nets.  There are a few possible sources, none of which are mutually exclusive, could be a bit of all of them :-P :
- learning rate a bit high => causes learning to diverge.  since the gradient surface is not planar or linear, but twisted and convoluted, this might not be apparent to start with ,and then, bam!
- a bug.  It's possible...
- all is perfectly in order, but for optimal learning you need some kind of gradient truncation, normalization, or regularization

There's no hard and fast rule or check to know which is which... I suppose what I would do is:
- look around for what are sensible learning rates for the model and data I'm using, slot those in.  does it work?
- if it's a novel model, first try on a less novel model, and then see step 1 above; ie try something simple first, get that working, then try the original research bit
- if you're using a standard-ish learning rate in a standardish model, and that model doesnt have any kind of additional regularization, normalization, gradient truncation etc that you dont have, then someone would need to dig a bit more.  'someone' probalyb menaing: you :-P

As far as 'digging a bit more', you're almost certainly need to roll up your sleeves and get stuck into the code, so I would try the first two steps first.  I thnk that to 'get stuck into the code', at  minimum, you'd probably want to do something like:
- arrange for the weights to be saved each iteration, to a different file each time
- as soon as it starts diverging, hitting nans, abort, note which iteration it was
- now you can load those weights directly, examine the weights, their magnitude etc.  what if you drop the learning rate?  are some weights heading for infinity?  will weight regularization help?  etc...

If it was me, I'd do this currenlty probalby using python. In the past, I would have done it directly in hte C++.

PS one thing you could try is, assume its a gpu kernel bug, so make everything run on the cpu, by modifying https://github.com/hughperkins/DeepCL/blob/master/src/conv/BackpropWeights.cpp#L51 to return `true` only for kernle 0 (ie the cpu kernel), and ditto for Forward, and Backward.  If this doesnt create NaNs, there might be a bug in one or more of the gpu kernels, for the specific geometry you are using.

I've added one more tweak, which is to use the natural ``tensordot`` output order of indices (input order: ``"zx,xy->zy"``) rather than ``einsum`` output order (alphabetic: ``"zx,xy->yz"``) for **intermediaries** only - doesn't effect output. 

The rationale is just to minimise transpositions. This doesn't really matter for numpy as transposing is lazy, but it will be helpful for some other libraries (e.g. https://github.com/springer13/tcl) since it maintains the array contiguity throughout.

I'm not following you Christian. When we call `stop()` from _thread 1_ an event is set and _thread 1_ blocks on the `join()` call waiting for _thread 2_ (which is executing `db_writer_thread()`).

At this point in _thread 2_ we are *probably* waiting in the middle of the blocking `get_message` calls. This returns either a message or `None` after the timeout `GET_MESSAGE_TIMEOUT` expires. In one case we write the received message to the sql database, in the other there was no message and when we get back to the top of the outer while loop we find out that we've been told to stop running.

What I assume was happening was that the test (in _thread 1_) was finished after the smaller sleep period expired while _thread 2_ was in it for the long haul - still happily waiting to buffer another message before it would write to the sql file.

```
    def db_writer_thread(self):
        num_frames = 0
        last_write = time.time()
        self._create_db()

        while not self.stop_running_event.is_set():
            messages = []

            m = self.get_message(self.GET_MESSAGE_TIMEOUT)
            while m is not None:
                log.debug("sqlitewriter buffering message")

                messages.append((
                    m.timestamp,
                    m.arbitration_id,
                    m.id_type,
                    m.is_remote_frame,
                    m.is_error_frame,
                    m.dlc,
                    buffer(m.data)
                ))
                m = self.get_message(self.GET_MESSAGE_TIMEOUT)

                if time.time() - last_write > self.MAX_TIME_BETWEEN_WRITES:
                    log.debug("Max timeout between writes reached")
                    break

            if len(messages) > 0:
                with self.conn:
                    log.debug("Writing %s frames to db", len(messages))
                    self.conn.executemany(SqliteWriter.insert_msg_template, messages)
                    num_frames += len(messages)
                    last_write = time.time()

        self.conn.close()
        log.info("Stopped sqlite writer after writing %s messages", num_frames)

    def stop(self):
        self.stop_running_event.set()
        log.debug("Stopping sqlite writer")
        self.writer_thread.join()
```

```python 


from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,
                        unicode_literals)

import argparse

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.indicators as btind


class SMAStrategy(bt.Strategy):
    params = (
        ('period', 10),
        ('onlydaily', False),
    )

    def __init__(self):
        self.sma_small_tf = btind.SMA(self.data, period=self.p.period)
        if not self.p.onlydaily:
            self.sma_large_tf = btind.SMA(self.data1, period=self.p.period)

    def nextstart(self):
        print('--------------------------------------------------')
        print('nextstart called with len', len(self))
        print('--------------------------------------------------')

        super(SMAStrategy, self).nextstart()


def runstrat():
    args = parse_args()

    # Create a cerebro entity
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro(stdstats=False)

    # Add a strategy
    if not args.indicators:
        cerebro.addstrategy(bt.Strategy)
    else:
        cerebro.addstrategy(
            SMAStrategy,

            # args for the strategy
            period=args.period,
            onlydaily=args.onlydaily,
        )

    # Load the Data
    datapath = args.dataname or '../../datas/2006-day-001.txt'
    data = btfeeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=datapath)
    cerebro.adddata(data)  # First add the original data - smaller timeframe

    tframes = dict(daily=bt.TimeFrame.Days, weekly=bt.TimeFrame.Weeks,
                   monthly=bt.TimeFrame.Months)

    # Handy dictionary for the argument timeframe conversion
    # Resample the data
    if args.noresample:
        datapath = args.dataname2 or '../../datas/2006-week-001.txt'
        data2 = btfeeds.BacktraderCSVData(dataname=datapath)
        # And then the large timeframe
        cerebro.adddata(data2)
    else:
        cerebro.resampledata(data, timeframe=tframes[args.timeframe],
                             compression=args.compression)

    #cerebro.addwriter(bt.WriterFile, csv=True)
    # Run over everything
    cerebro.run()

    # Plot the result
    cerebro.plot(style='bar')


def parse_args():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description='Multitimeframe test')

    parser.add_argument('--dataname', default='', required=False,
                        help='File Data to Load')

    parser.add_argument('--dataname2', default='', required=False,
                        help='Larger timeframe file to load')

    parser.add_argument('--noresample', action='store_true',
                        help='Do not resample, rather load larger timeframe')

    parser.add_argument('--timeframe', default='weekly', required=False,
                        choices=['daily', 'weekly', 'monhtly'],
                        help='Timeframe to resample to')

    parser.add_argument('--compression', default=1, required=False, type=int,
                        help='Compress n bars into 1')

    parser.add_argument('--indicators', action='store_true',
                        help='Wether to apply Strategy with indicators')

    parser.add_argument('--onlydaily', action='store_true',
                        help='Indicator only to be applied to daily timeframe')

    parser.add_argument('--period', default=10, required=False, type=int,
                        help='Period to apply to indicator')

    return parser.parse_args()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    runstrat()

But to be sure, I've included some new unit tests for cases where `d=0` and `out_of_sample > 0`. See, for instance, `pyramid/arima/tests/test_arima.test_oob_for_issue_29`:

```python
# Test Issue #29 (d=0, cv=True) -----------------------------------------------
def test_oob_for_issue_29():
    y = np.array([3.0, 4.0, 1.0, 2.0, 1.0, 2.0, 0.0, 1.0,
                  0.0, 0.0, 2.0, 0.0, 1.0, 1.0, 2.0, 2.0])

    xreg = np.random.RandomState(1).rand(y.shape[0], 3)

    # Try for cv on/off, various D levels, and various Xregs
    for d in (0, 1):
        for cv in (0, 3):
            for exog in (xreg, None):

                # surround with try/except so we can log the failing combo
                try:
                    model = ARIMA(order=(1, d, 1),
                                  out_of_sample_size=cv).fit(y, exogenous=exog)

                    # If exogenous is defined, we need to pass n_periods of
                    # exogenous rows to the predict function. Otherwise we'll
                    # just leave it at None
                    if exog is not None:
                        xr = exog[:3, :]
                    else:
                        xr = None

                    _, _ = model.predict(n_periods=3, return_conf_int=True,
                                         exogenous=xr)

                except Exception:
                    print("Failing combo: d=%i, cv=%i, exog=%r"
                          % (d, cv, exog))
                    raise
```

It depends on how you use it. https://ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg-filters.html#trim

Anyway this doesn't look like `ffmpeg-python`'s duty, this is an `ffmpeg` issue. `ffmpeg-python` does not have access to information about a running stream.

The only thing it can do to *stop a stream after X seconds* is killing the process and you can do it in many ways, such as starting a timer in a separate thread, blocking the main thread for X seconds or scheduling a callback after X seconds in any asynchronous framework you're using (like twisted or py3's coroutine stuff).

from aiohttp import ClientSession

class Client:
    _client = None

        self._client = ClientSession()

    @property
    def cli(self):
        return self._client

    async def __aenter__(self):
        return self._client

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        pass

    def close(self):
        self._client.close()

I added a support for Django 1.7 at a time where abstract model were required to have an `app_label` to prevent a deprecation warning. The thing is this bound all subclasses to the `'polymodels'` app instead of the one they were defined in.

Since `'polymodels'` doesn't have any migration it must explicitly be specified to `makemigrations` if you want changes to be detected, hence the `'No changes detected'` message you're getting.

Fix and new release are coming.

Maybe there is a way to split it after a specific commit? ``5f51c7ff`` was the last commit really dealing with class-based namespaces. Up to that commit, everything should be clear.

The following 5 commits (everything that came later) deal with the concept of event handler middlewares. I wrote a chapter in the docs about them. Basically it's a way to add before/after event handlers on three layers for fine-grained control: These layers can alter the arguments the event handler will get or its return value transparently or completely abort execution, returning something else.

- at server layer for *every* event by adding the middleware to ``socketio.Server().middlewares``
- at namespace layer by adding the middleware to ``socketio.Namespace``
- at the event handler layer by decorating an event handler with ``@util.apply_middleware(MW)``

Finally, when the event is triggered, the ``socketio.Server()._trigger_event`` method collects metadata for the used event handler from these three layers (from least to most specific one) and applies them on the handler before execution.

I wrote a test for it that is commented and it works well.

Hopefully it won't get lost after all because it is so simple and powerful.

@corndogcomputers 
In the first case you provide it could be like this

    print(droplet.get_events()) # use get_events everytime
    print(droplet.get_events()) # use get_events everytime

There's nothing wrong with your second example. In fact this refactoring

    for droplet in manager.droplets:
        for event in droplet.events:
            print(event.id)

will work as expected and it is equivalent to

    for droplet in manager.get_all_droplets():
        for event in droplet.get_events():
            print event.id

The problem comes in when developers use droplet.events multiple times expecting the same result, expecting it to work like the other droplet attributes like `.name` `.size` etc. It's a matter of consistency.


This code is fine
    
    pairs = []
    for i in range(len(droplets.snapshot_ids) - 1):
        new_pair = (droplets.snapshot_ids[i], droplets.snapshot_ids[i + 1])
        pairs.append(new_pair)

where as this code

    pairs = []
    for i in range(len(droplets.snapshots) - 1):
        new_pair = (droplets.snapshots[i], droplets.snapshots[i + 1])
        pairs.append(new_pair)

will be slow (and incorrect if snapshots changes while this loop is running).

You're right it is a matter of preference and python is a very opinionated language (or maybe it's just me :P). To quote from the (Zen of Python)[https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/].

    Explicit is better than implicit.

    There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

`get_events()` and `events` do the same thing, there should only be one and `get_events()` is more explicit and `events`.

Than you. As far as I can see `my_models.filter(pgp_pub_field='h')` will not work as you have to explicitly use the `decrypted` lookup to query the decrypted value of the field.

```python
>>> my_models.filter(pgp_pub_field__decrypted='h')
>>> my_models.filter(pgp_pub_field__decrypted='haa')
>>> my_models.filter(pgp_pub_field__decrypted='1111')
```
The 3 querysets above can work if any of your two instances `<QuerySet [<MyModel: #1>, <MyModel: #2>]>` have either `h`, `haa` or `1111` as values.

I can see that your first object has `'hello, world!\r\n안녕하세요.'` as value for `pgp_pub_field`. You can then filter with the following and you should have an object back:

```python
>>> my_models.filter(pgp_pub_field__decrypted='hello, world!\r\n안녕하세요.')
```

Copied from a [previous discussion](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/pull/1120#issuecomment-342686339):

What I'm planning to do is something like what I [suggested on #1104](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/issues/1104#issuecomment-341847301). More specifically, I'd add a new command, maybe called `install_kernel`. This would behave and have options similar to [`python -m ipykernel install`](https://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install/kernel_install.html#kernels-for-different-environments)  and the [bash kernel](https://github.com/takluyver/bash_kernel) with respect to installing a kernel spec, but would add the `django_extensions` notebook extension. I'd try to have helpful defaults, like defaulting to `--user` with a name that reflects the active virtual environment.

With the kernel spec installed, users would be free to run `jupyter notebook`, `jupyter lab`, `jupyter qtconsole`, etc. in whatever environment they want (their Django virtualenv, a Jupyter virtualenv, Anaconda, etc.). And, `django_extensions` wouldn't need the `NOTEBOOK_ARGUMENTS` and `IPYTHON_ARGUMENTS` settings, because it's no longer acting as a wrapper around the `notebook` command, and users would be free to edit the installed kernel spec as needed.

We'd use documentation to introduce the new command and recommend a switch, then later deprecating `--notebook` and `--kernel`, and eventually removing them.

Testing this with the latest 17.0 release as well as 16.0.3, I find:
 - setting `REQ_CORRELATE` and`REQ_RELAXED` works fine, and the settings appear to take effect properly (e.g. with `REQ_RELAXED` I can `send` multiple requests without interleaving `recv` calls.) This works either with `socket.setsockopt(zmq.REQ_CORRELATE, True)` or `socket.REQ_CORRELATE = True`.
 - however, getting these options (via `val = socket.getsockopt(zmq.REQ_CORRELATE)` or just `val = socket.REQ_CORRELATE` raises an error.

The error raised in the former case (using `getsockopt`) is:
```
In [33]: s.getsockopt(zmq.REQ_CORRELATE)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ZMQError                                  Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-33-c7406ed2575f> in <module>()
----> 1 s.getsockopt(zmq.REQ_CORRELATE)

ZMQError: Invalid argument
```
The error in the latter case (using the socket attr) is similar.

I can't find an obvious source for the error. These constants are type `int` in the ZMQ documents, and the code path in `zmq/backend/cython/socket.pyx` seems to calling `zmq_getsockopt` assuming an `int` type. Does the underlying zmq library incorrectly set an error condition? Or is `zmq.backend.cython.checkrc._check_rc` incorrectly detecting an error that isn't there? Getting other int-type socket options such as RCVTIMEO works fine, so it's not a general problem.

Thanks @technige for the quick response. In previous versions of the driver we would get the warning though in 1.5.0 the exception is raised.

Referring to the code from 1.4.0 (https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/1.4/neo4j/v1/security.py) if SSL_AVAILABLE is False this implies that ENCRYPTION_DEFAULT = 0, which means that the encrypted = 0 (assuming that the encrypted key in the config is undefined or None). The warning is displayed and the if statement on line 63 evaluates to False.

In 1.5.0 assuming the same configuration, it seems the new default_config is used if the encrypted config key is undefined which defaults to True (https://github.com/neo4j/neo4j-python-driver/blob/1.5/neo4j/config.py#L52) and thus the warning is no longer displayed and the if statement evaluates to True causing the exception to be thrown.

@mauritsvanrees in the original commit collective/i18ndude@1b73cb152efd8d3c9944ef37cf9a4dfab2dd26b2 I wrote that when using Chameleon, it's necessary to update the i18n.WHITELIST: 

```
# Make it a non-frozen set.
chameleon.i18n.WHITELIST = set(chameleon.i18n.WHITELIST)
chameleon.i18n.WHITELIST.update(('ignore', 'ignore-attributes'))
```

This comment didn't make it into the changes of the i18ndude release and I was too lazy to do a pull request on chameleon. Thank you @gforcada for fixing this properly!

This isn't a great example, because it doesn't show what kind of starts
and ends you are inserting. From the output you post I'm pretty sure
they are not localized, but floating.

If you do something like this:

```
LONDON = pytz.timezone('Europe/London')
event.add('dtstart', LONDON.localize(dt.datetime.now()))
```
you should end up with something that looks like this:

```
DTSTART;TZID=Europe/London;VALUE=DATE-TIME:20170531TXXYYZZ .
```

BUT.... icalendar does not actually create VTIMEZONE components for you,
you'll have to do that for yourself. If you really need to do this, you
might want to have a look at https://github.com/pimutils/khal 's code
base and see how we do it there (grep for VTIMEZONE).

If you are lucky, google might actually interpret a
TZID=$olsondb_identifier as you intended, but it might also discard that
completely.

Unrelated note: you should set DTSTAMP to the current time, not the end
of the event.

@Midnighter I did some more research and under the hood it seems that when doing an orphan checkout the branch is not actually created. The branch will only exist after the first git commit in which case the refs/heads/<orphan-branch> will point to the newly created commit with no parents.

I think the way to go is to catch the exception.
Exceptions are the way GitPython communicates errors in most (if not all) cases, and I believe there is no way around it.

@techalchemy Trying to use pipenv in a multiuser environment (HPC cluster) and `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` works well enough for creation, but if this variable is not set during use, pipenv isn't smart enough to realize there's a .venv directory and creates another virtual env. It completely ignores the pipfile/pipfile.lock and doesn't install any of the packages (besides pip, setuptools, and wheel).
 
Pipenv doesn't allow easy sharing of projects between users unless: 

- The virtualenv path can be named during creation

   - Or make it local with a command line flag instead of/or in addition to the `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` env var.

- The project stores the virtualenv path inside the Pipfile/Pipfile.lock or automatically detects a .venv directory.

There's no way I can have hundreds of users creating their own virtual environments and reinstalling all packages in their home directories on first use of every python app. If I enter a directory pipenv should know what and where to find everything (regardless of user.)

One or more developers might create a project but once deployed, it might be run by many users with various environment variables set, running multiple python applications at once with different dependencies. As you can see, venv in project is a major feature, without which, pipenv is practically unusable for end users who are actually running these apps. 

As you said: 

> the goal is more to move away from working inside / caring about the location of the environment

- How can a user do this if the virtualenv with all of the compiled python packages is in a developer's home directory instead of a central location with the app? 
- If the virtualenv is in the central location, how can the user do this if pipenv ignores .venv and the pipfiles?

> Just please don't have a boolean flag in the parameters to the function.

I'm fine with it being a separate function. Normally I'm not a big fan of a bunch of proliferating boolean parameters, but I think this is a borderline case. It's fundamentally the same function used in one of two modes. All the rest of the input parameters are the same and have the same effects and the result is the same (conceptually). This sort of "mode" flag is just providing information about whether this is a package with implicit namespaces or not in the same way that `include` tells you where the base package locations are and `exclude` tells you what parts are not part of the package.

In any case, if you're fundamentally against a boolean parameter I don't see a problem with keeping it separate. I've been promoting using [alternate constructor-style variants](https://github.com/python-variants/variants) lately because I prefer `find_packages.namespace` to `find_packages_namespace`, but `find_package_ns` works too.

I’m uncertain how there would be a problem with untagged (i.e., `?` non-specific tagged) mappings. In the schema proposed above, untagged (`?` non-specific tagged) mappings would *always* resolve to plain JavaScript objects. However, `!` non-specific tagged mappings would *always* resolve to `tag:yaml.org,2002:map`, which would correspond to JavaScript `Map`s (in this schema).

Indeed, the function of the `!` non-specific tag is to “quote” a node, ‘allow[ing] the author of a YAML character stream to effectively “disable” the tag resolution process. By explicitly specifying a “!” non-specific tag property, the node would then be resolved to a “vanilla” sequence, mapping, or string, according to its kind.’). No dynamic auto-detection based on keys or whatever would be required.

As far as I can tell, the above would be deterministic, and round-trip conversion would be lossless. It would be just another YAML schema.

Furthermore, there would be no performance regression for existing code: the schema would be opt-in; the default schema would remain `DEFAULT_SAFE_SCHEMA`. `DEFAULT_SAFE_SCHEMA` would still resolve both `?`- and `!`-non-specifically-tagged mappings (as well as all other mappings with tags resolved into `tag:yaml.org,2002:map`) as plain JavaScript objects.

My apologies if I’m misunderstanding something. Thanks in advance, and thank you again for js-yaml.

@zpincus

Wow.

Thank you for that amazing response.

You are, of course, right about your assessment that this whole confusion, *my* whole confusion, has to do with display of the arrays. That is, both Matplotlib and NumPy printing works in row/column order, and that had coloured my whole view about our coordinate systems. As you say, there's a few different issues mixed up here and they were all jumbled in my head until your post. (They are probably still *a bit* jumbled, but much less so. ;)

So, let me clarify, in light of this new understanding, why some parts of skimage are still incorrect: in parts of the `transform` module, coordinate [x, y] refers not to the coordinates x on axis0 and y axis1 of a NumPy array (that may or may not be displayed incorrectly by matplotlib). Rather, they refer to `array[y, x]`. See, for example, the `dst` coordinates in [this example](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/xx_applications/plot_geometric.html#parameter-estimation).

Do you agree that this is bad?

*That* is the primary problem that I tried to address in moving to `r, c` notation. I hope you agree that this example is problematic, even in the context of x, y coordinates. What your example shows is that it is not the notation that is wrong, but rather the axis/coordinate ordering.

Anyway, I think I'm going to put your vote down for "M, N, P". =P In my opinion, "N0, N1, N2" is too unwieldy for a docstring.

(Regarding scanline efficiency, that remains a problem with x/y, but you are right that it is a much more minor point.)

Digging into the problem a bit more, my buffer error was caused because `traceback._print` was called with a `str` argument of `u'    transaction.commit()'`, being the last line run in ipython.  So perhaps ipython does something different with its framing (eg `sys._getframe` at https://github.com/zopefoundation/transaction/blob/master/transaction/_transaction.py#L330).

    forfait_logement = simulation.calculate('rsa_forfait_logement', referencePeriod)

I am not against adding parameters to function. In this case, however you can find a way to keep a testable solution although an ugly one: you can define ppa_fictive_m_1, ppa_fictive_m_2 and ppa_fictive_m_3  and they share all three the same reference period.

````python
def function(self, simulation, period, fictive_period = None):
    period = period.this_month
    fictive_period = fictive_period if fictive_period else period
    forfait_logement = simulation.calculate('rsa_forfait_logement', period)
````

Hi @fpagnoux, thanks for your feedback on this issue. Please find my comments below.

> Would that mean than a build would use 2 of our 4 containers ?

The way we define parallelism in v2 has changed. From https://circleci.com/gh/openfisca/openfisca-core/edit#parallel-builds _these settings are only for 1.0 builds. To define parallelism for 2.0 jobs [read our documentation](https://circleci.com/docs/2.0/configuration-reference/#jobs)._

Parallelism is defined now is at the *job* level. What I'm advocating for is for a [_Fan-Out/Fan-In_](https://circleci.com/docs/2.0/workflows/#fan-outfan-in-workflow-example) *workflow* strategy, not to parallelise jobs.

> Also, unless there is a significant issue with the current state of the code I didn't see, I'm really dubious about the relevance of requesting changes 5 days after a PR has been accepted, based on what seems to be a minor issue.

I did my best effort to give a review based on what I thought was the expected behaviour @Anna-Livia was looking for, and based on my current knowledge of the v2.

Is the very same reason why I also included an example https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/compare/python3...python3-circleci?expand=1

Also, see that at the time of my review [tests were failing](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/pull/645#issuecomment-387738793).

Additionally, by the way cache is being used in the current implementation, they'll likely be errors as we're mixing up caches from different containers.

Finally, note that the current configuration hides a misconfiguration dependency: deployment should depend on both v2.7 *and* v3.6, and it is not, but this is obfuscated by the fact of the jobs running sequentially.

> Open to discuss running Py2 and Py3 tests in parallel, but this does not seem to be a good reason to not merge this PR that has been opened for a month now.

Pull Requests that rotten are a far more serious issue than this, so there's no problem.

For the next time, though, I'd suggest that :

- it be the OP that reacts to the review, dismissing it if found out of context or disproportionate
- if we're going to bypass a review, we verify all information to be accurate *before*

Bypassing a review is not light matter, and should not become a regular practice. It *must* be solid ground.

@resmo a proposition avoiding too much changes.

| context | behavior |
| ------- | ------------- |
| complete env | read only the envs (current behavior) |
| complete ini | read only the ini (current behavior) |
| incomplete ini | read the ini, then enrich it with envs |

What it doesn't let you do, is to override a value from the `ini`. An idea could be to keep : ini then enrich with a way to discard some values from the `ini`. E.g. one could deactivate the `verify` value and replace the endpoint and secret from the provided `.ini` file.

```bash
export CLOUDSTACK_IGNORE_FIELDS="endpoint,secret,verify"
export CLOUDSTACK_ENDPOINT="..."
export CLOUDSTACK_SECRET="..."
```

I've finished PR #434 which implements the new layout for the **Grid** class and the NetCDF files that Py-ART uses to store these objects.  I made a long write-up on the changes in that PR.  If anyone wants to review the PR I would welcome and greatly appreciate it.

@gamaanderson  and @tjlang and other who may need to change their code to make use of the new layout should know about the `Grid.from_legacy_parameter` class method which uses the older "legacy" Grid parameters to create a **Grid** object.  Additionally, the `read_legacy_grid` method can be used to read in NetCDF files which use the legacy variable names and the **convert_legacy_grid** script will convert these files to the new format.  No support is included for creating NetCDF files with the legacy variable names.

Hopefully this will make the transition easier, although I know it will still require some changes.  I do think they new layout is more informative and will offer flexibility down the road.

Seeing everything implemented, I'm in favor of moving the depreciation schedule of the *axes* attribute and the legacy grid functions back a bit.  They are not conflicting with any of the new layout so I think including them in the next two major release(1.6.0 and 1.7.0) will not be a problem.  

It would be possible to never remove these legacy Grid functions and the *axes* attribute but I do not like this option.  Having multiple methods for access the same data and multiple file formats is not a good long term solution.   To Quote from the Zen of Python: 

```
There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.
```

Py-ART will now decompress gzip and bzip2 files passed to the `read_nexrad_archive` function.  Adding this functionality directly to the **NEXRADLevel2File** and related classes would introduced undesired coupling in this module or require extensive repetition of code, but of which are bad.  Py-ART does expose the `prepare_for_read` function in the pyart.io namespace which can be used to add this functionality to the **NEXRADLevel2File** or other classes.  For example:

``` Python
import pyart
from pyart.io.nexrad_level2 import NEXRADLevel2File
nfile = NEXRADLevel2File(pyart.io.prepare_for_read('KATX20130717_195021_V06.gz'))
```

Additionally the `prepare_for_read` function can be easily exacted from Py-ART and included directly in the nexrad_level2.py module if so desired.

I believe this to be the best solution to this issue, although it is not exactly what was requested.

I have struggled with this issue as well. My work around has normally been to use policy documents for my lambda functions instead.

That way we are able to create policy documents for our functions that contains intrinsic functions.

```yml
  Function:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
      Handler: index.handler
      CodeUri: .
      Events:
        GetResource:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /
            Method: get
      Policies:
        - Version: '2012-10-17'
          Statement:
            - Effect: Allow
              Action: dynamodb:Query
              Resource: !Sub 'arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${Table}'
      Environment:
        Variables:
          TableName: !Ref Table
```

If you're using layers, than you're building a charm layer - doesn't matter if it has a hook directory, it's only a fragment of a charm. In which case you need to use `charm build` and you are writing a layer. A charm is either written from scratch or compile from a series of layers. If you look, for example, at the base layer, you'll see it has hooks and other file/folder fragements. Layers are not limited to just the reactive directory - in fact reactive framework and charm layers are not mutually exclusive.

In short, this is a layer despite what you may feel, and you should include a `repo` key to point to where the source for this layer is :wink:

`--net=none` and `network_disabled` are two different things. The command you're running with docker-py is actually equivalent to `docker run --networking=false debian:sid cat /etc/hosts`

```
$ docker run -t --networking=false ubuntu cat /etc/hosts 
Warning: '--networking' is deprecated, it will be removed soon. See usage.
$ docker run -t --net=none ubuntu cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1	localhost
::1	localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0	ip6-localnet
ff00::0	ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1	ip6-allnodes

@gerred a few responses:

1. In offline discussion with you and @helgi, it seems the favored approach is to only consider the proximate protocol.  So we can try to match a regex that looks something like this: `^(?:.*,)?https$`

2. > As of now, we've only found two frameworks that do this

  I've found more mentions of comma delimited `X-Forwarded-Proto` since our initial discussion.  Having said that, I think amending these headers is one of the harder aspects of this problem.  I think I should attempt this, but not burn too much time on it.

3. If the actual protocol on an inbound request _is_ HTTPS, I think that should trump whatever the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header says when it comes to determining a value of the `$access_scheme` variable-- which ultimately dictates whether the current request is treated as secure or not.  This sounds reasonable, but it's not what happens today.  Technically, this may be a _separate_ bug, but if the logic that determines the value of `$access_scheme` is about to be re-written, it just makes sense to squash these two bugs together.

4. I want to try and avoid Lua.  If it's unavoidable, so be it, but it's possible we can achieve "good enough" without it.  No. 2 above is the bit that was most likely to have needed it, and that's the bit I'm most likely to not mess with if it gives me too much grief.

I added this method to generate sql without with, select and limit clauses.
```
    def get_count_sql(self, debug=False, use_cache=True):
        """
        Generates the sql for this query and returns the sql as a string.

        :type debug: bool
        :param debug: If True, the sql will be returned in a format that is easier to read and debug.
            Defaults to False

        :type use_cache: bool
        :param use_cache: If True, the query will returned the cached sql if it exists rather
            then generating the sql again. If False, the sql will be generated again. Defaults to True.

        :rtype: str
        :return: The generated sql for this query
        """
        # TODO: enable caching
        # if self.sql and use_cache and not debug:
        # return self.sql

        # auto alias any naming collisions
        self.check_name_collisions()

        # if debugging, return the debug formatted sql
        if debug:
            return self.format_sql()

        # build each part of the query
        sql = ''
        sql += "SELECT COUNT(*) as count "
        sql += self.build_from_table()
        sql += self.build_joins()
        sql += self.build_where()
        sql += self.build_groups()

        # remove any whitespace from the beginning and end of the sql
        self.sql = sql.strip()

        return self.sql
```

Cloned and modified select method:
```
def select_count(self, sql=None, sql_args=None):
        """
        Executes the SELECT  COUNT(*) statement and returns the result

        :type sql: str or None
        :param sql: The sql to execute in the SELECT statement. If one is not specified, then the
            query will use ``self.get_sql()``

        :type sql_args: str or None
        :param sql_args: The sql args to be used in the SELECT statement. If none are specified, then
            the query wil use ``self.get_args()``

        :rtype: list of dict
        :return: integer
        """

        # determine which sql to use
        if sql is None:
            sql = self.get_count_sql()

        # determine which sql args to use
        if sql_args is None:
            sql_args = self.get_args()

        # get the cursor to execute the query
        cursor = self.get_cursor()

        # execute the query
        cursor.execute(sql, sql_args)

        # get the results as a list of dictionaries
        result = cursor.fetchone()

        return result[0]
```

 def count(self, field='*'):
        """
        Returns a COUNT of the query by wrapping the query and performing a COUNT
        aggregate of the specified field

        :param field: the field to pass to the COUNT aggregate. Defaults to '*'
        :type field: str

        :return: The number of rows that the query will return
        :rtype: int
        """
        return self.select_count()

Gladly. :)

If an executable is outputting anything that *isn't* logs, then it's convenient to separate the two streams (executable output & logs). Here, that comes up in `trinity console` which outputs the result of your console commands, and the logs. The console is essentially unusable unless you turn logging down to `error`, because it's too noisy. Even then, it's pretty annoying to have text output into your console session. Splitting the streams makes it easy to do:

```sh
trinity console 2>trinity.log
```

Then, you get only the console output in your terminal, and your logs are viewable in the `trinity.log` file.

> So, I'm not clear on when we came to the conclusion that it was right to minimize features. Maybe you can clarify your reasoning here or maybe I have simply missed it on one of the many threads. :)

The reason we need to minimize the number of features is that some packages have multiple `track_features` possibilities, and we need to make sure that no more than one of those is selected. This is particularly critical for Windows, where Python itself relies on features to control the MSVC runtime selection. It is very unlikely this is going to change, therefore.

The problem right now is that while _you_ are adding the `openblas` track_feature to the `openblas` package, the default channel is not. During a `conda create ... nanshe`, `nanshe` has a choice: install a version with the feature, and install a version without it. It is going to prefer to install the feature-less version of the package.

Now, it is true that your OpenBLAS has a later version number than default; that's true. However, _you didn't ask to maximize the version_ of `openblas`. The order of preference here is this:

1. Maximize the versions of _requested_ packages (nanshe)
2. Minimize the version count (mkl is selected, but not openblas)
3. Maximize the number of packages with the selected features
4. Maximize the version numbers of the packages

On the other hand, when you do `conda update --all`, you _are_ asking to maximize the version of OpenBLAS; and when you do that, you get the `openblas` track_feature. That's why `conda update --all` is producing a different solution than the initial `conda create`.

The solution here, in my view, is clear: remove the `openblas` track_feature from your builds of OpenBLAS and put it somewhere else. I'm recommending `nanshe` itself, but you don't have to do that if you don't want to. But it needs to be somewhere where you can guarantee it gets pulled in by `nanshe`.

Another solution is to hard-code the OpenBLAS version into your `nanshe` recipe, so that it makes sure to pull in your build of OpenBLAS. But that is fragile, in that it might break the minute the default channel updates OpenBLAS.

Here is the post-link script:

```
setlocal enabledelayedexpansion
REM This file should be run after installing the qt package into any
REM    environment.  It makes it so Qt knows where to look for includes and
REM    libs.  This script is copied in bld.bat to the post-link script for qt,
REM    which means it is run after the Qt package gets linked, and sets up
REM    the correct path for whatever Conda env you install Qt to.
pushd "%~dp0\..\"
set "FORWARD_SLASHED_PREFIX=%CD:\=/%"
if not exist "%CD%\Library" mkdir "%CD%\Library"
if not exist "%CD%\Library\bin" mkdir "%CD%\Library\bin"
echo [Paths] > "%CD%\Library\bin\qt.conf"
echo Prefix = %FORWARD_SLASHED_PREFIX%/Library >> "%CD%\Library\bin\qt.conf"
REM some things go looking in the prefix root (pyqt, for example)
COPY %CD%\Library\bin\qt.conf %CD%\qt.conf
```

@ilanschnell:

> 1) Which specific current problem does this CEP solve?
> 2) What is the objective (other than changing many things)?

Here's an abbreviated version of [this][1] inside the CEP.

[1]: https://github.com/conda/conda/wiki/CEP-1:-Replacing-root-environment-with-default-environment#technical-simplification

* pip installation is hard because of `sys.prefix`
* mixing managing an installation of `conda` (the package) and managing a conda installation (the environments and packages) makes updating on Windows hard
* we have special case code for conda that other code can't opt-in and can't be overridden (see #1128)
* testing packages that rely on conda have to circumvent conda's built in checks
* activation logic is more brittle than it needs to be because it has to remove as well as add values
* there's no way to deactivate a Miniconda / Anaconda root install and still keep `conda` on the command line
* we're providing user's a method of using Anaconda that will eventually make it harder for them to use by not putting them in an environment that's separate from conda at the outset

But the driving motivation behind this is that there is no way to ensure that I'm looking at the same conda installation that `conda info` is without running that command (or reading `head -n1 $(which conda)` doing `dirname(dirname())` on it.

As a side note, the parenthetical comment makes it seem as though I'm simply changing things for the sake of change.  I'm disappointed that you would think I would suggest such a non-trivial change just for the sake of change itself. :-(

> 3) How would the installers (Miniconda, Anaconda) have to change?

These are still kind of a black box so I can't say with 100% certainty.  At a very meta level:

* Unpack / install conda
* Initial conda installation
* Populate the package cache in that system
* Create the default environment

> 4) Would cas-installer have to change as well?

I don't believe so.  `cas-installer` is meant to be used absent Conda, correct?  If so, there's no reason for it to change.

> 5) What would migration look like? (We have to ensure conda update conda still works.)

This is actually the reason to add a `.conda-initialized` file or some such.  We can use that to detect whether it's been upgraded, then prompt the user to upgrade.  The upgrade process would essentially:

* Run the new `conda init` to initialize the system environment (not sure what all that would entail until we start fleshing out the code)
* Use the history file to reconstruct a new default env (failing a history file, look for `anaconda` and use that which assumes nothing new was installed)
* Remove existing packages from the root/system environment that are not supposed to be there (for example: all packages without type `system`)
* Prompt the user to include the new `source activate` code and offer an option to add it ourselves where we can.

This migration should be optional for a long time which would give us a long time to get it figured out correctly.

Hi, I have studied this case a bit now. The problem here is the setup of the analysis with 2 children only. There are a lot of compounds in the raw analysis, problem is that when there are only two children all variants that are heterozygous in the affected and hom ref in healthy will be annotated as AD_dn and scored based on this. The AR and AR_comp variants will be pushed down in the list and this explains why we do not see them.
If one or both parents where present the vast majority of the AD_dn variants would be downgraded and not seen at all.

You just mount the configuration as a volume in the gixy container and give it the path to the config file as an argument. For example:

```
λ ~/ docker run --rm -v `pwd`/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf yandex/gixy:latest /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
[nginx_parser]  WARNING File not found: /etc/nginx/conf/mime.types

==================== Results ===================

==================== Summary ===================
Total issues:
    Unspecified: 0
    Low: 0
    Medium: 0
    High: 0
```

In my case, I'm already using docker-compose in my CI pipeline, and in that case you can use `volumes_from` to mount the configuration from your nginx container to your gixy container:

```
version: '2'
services:
  nginx:
    image: nginx:alpine
    volumes:
      - /etc/nginx

  scanner:
    image: yandex/gixy:latest
    volumes_from:
      - nginx
    command: "/etc/nginx/nginx.conf"
    depends_on:
      - nginx
```

Then just `docker-compose up -d nginx && docker-compose run --rm scanner`

I think we're talking past each other a bit here. In v1, `instance.to_native()` produces a dictionary of native scalars. In v2.dev it produces a model instance that has been put through the export machinery; this is to apply roles or whatever there might be.

In v2.dev, `instance.to_dict()` is the equivalent of the old `instance.to_native()`.

The reasoning had its origins in the old codebase where `to_native` would appear in `ModelType` & co, where it would handle data import. In other words, the *native form of a model* was strongly implied to be a Model instance, not a dictionary. It looked like a bug that `to_native()` would produce an instance in one code path but a dictionary in another.

But #368 addressed the conceptual issue by introducing the import/export methods, which are properly orthogonal to native/primitive concerns. So now I think returning a dictionary from `instance.to_native()` does make sense, as it's an export operation. Thus, I'm considering going back to the old behavior and getting rid of `to_dict()`.
FYI: v2.0.1 just released with these changes.

Your diagnosis is quite correct. I thought I explained this in my [first reply](https://github.com/schematics/schematics/issues/386#issuecomment-179975912).

The import is there for a reason: to ensure modules are imported in a predefined order. This, in turn, is to ensure that patches don't inadvertently introduce circular dependencies that only crop up when a particular module is imported first.

> `tests_require` is still a valid and widely used approach

Can you point to any evidence of this?

I looked at a few major projects (Django, SQLAlchemy, Flask), and, of those, only SQLAlchemy still includes `tests_require` in addition to its tox config. And it has to resort to [this kind of mess](https://github.com/zzzeek/sqlalchemy/blob/master/setup.py#L110) to extract the version number.

`tests_require` is also not mentioned *at all* in the [Python Packaging User Guide](http://python-packaging-user-guide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/), which is the most current resource.

That said, I'll probably solve this one way or the other before 2.0.0 is out. Have you by any chance noticed that 2.0.0.dev1 is a *development* release? This illustrates why relying on obsolete tools is a problem — your test script shouldn't even be trying to install it by default because it's a pre-release.

The motivation for replacing register_box/register_vspace with Box.register/VSpace.register was to unclutter the imports. There might also be an argument that we expect object/class methods to possibly mutate the object/class, whereas we expect regular functions to be pure (although nobody will be surprised that 'register_X' has side effects). I'm not sure I understand why it matters whether we call it once or more than once, but we do call e.g. [ArrayVSpace.register](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/c36319aaf8d0b38f5c9dd2c0189561c94d03043e/autograd/numpy/numpy_vspaces.py#L62) multiple times. Looking at it again, I think I agree that `register_vspace(DictVSpace, dict)` might be clearer than `DictVSpace.register(dict)`. A compromise would be `VSpace.register(DictVSpace, dict)`, where `VSpace` is used as a namespace and to give a hint as to what's being mutated.

@joel-coffman Correct. `time.gmtime` does not produce a string, it produces a `struct_time`. That struct would need to be fed through `time.asctime` (preferable since it is not supposed to be affected by the locale) or `time.strftime` (which is affected by the locale) to get a human readable string. Using `time.asctime(time.gmtime(...))` should address the issue in #124 and keep us immune from locale language issues down the road, if I've read the `time` docs correctly.

If locale language persists as a problem though, we will have two options: (1) verify that `DateTime.__str__` produces a string but ignore its actual value; or (2) dynamically generate the expected time string like the current patch, taking into account the change from `time.ctime` to `time.asctime(time.gmtime(...))`.

I believe I have addressed the problem.  The default date form is generally the Teradata format (date stored as an integer), but I think in your case the Teradata server has a default of ANSI date (the 10 character string ISO date).  The Teradata CLIv2 documentation indicates that Teradata format is always available, while ANSI is not necessarily supported on all systems (although my guess is that it probably will be available on almost all systems).  With that in mind, I'm explicitly setting the date form to be used to Teradata, which will override the default ANSI date and return the date that is already expected by giraffez. > each time the consumer hits a fresh end offset

ok, let me freely admit that I probably don't fully understand this sentence.

If we rewind back a little, as a Developer, I would like my `Consumer` to know whether there are still messages in the queue, and if so fetch them and do something with them; or not, in which case I can `sleep()` or work on other stuff (I am writing an `asyncio`-compatible `KafkaListener` so not blocking is imperative).

For this purpose, in my (admittedly, limited) experimentation, the `_EOF` "error" is eminently useless - the only alternative that appears to be viable is to have a very short (possibly 0) timeout in the `poll()` call and check for `None` as the return value - and just ignore the `_EOF` messages as just an endearing quirk of the kafka python client.

>If the produce rate is less than fetch.wait.max.ms it is likely that there will be an EOF event for each produced message.

Of course, but what if the messages are already in the queue? Although my Producer (by design) only produces a message at a very leisurely pace of one every 3 seconds, it started long before the Consumer went to fetch them: so there are, I don't know, say 100-120 sitting there - and, yet, every other fetched message results in a `_EOF` error.

It is entirely possible that this is "intended behavior" (although, I'm kinda hard-pressed to understand its usefulness) but shouldn't it be documented somewhere?

There are 3 issues really reported here:

1. When you get a `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute ‘read'`, it should not happen. In such case, we should handle it gracefully and retry... Fixing it now by a specific code for such issue does not make much sense as this issue is specific to urllib2, which we are now switching for `requests`. That will solve this issue and other problems along with it.

1. Connection reset: sever-side issue. B2 service is in beta and from time to time it does something crazy like close a connection without an apparent reason. Backblaze is working fast on resolving those issues as their service matures, so in the future it their servers are less and less likely to become overloaded and you should not see those. As you said, this problem was temporary and their ops team seems to have taken care of it rather quickly.

1. `BadStatusLine`: same as 2.

Alternatively, you could add object to the Schema's context so that it is accessible to your `post_load` handler:

```python
class BSchema(Schema):
    @post_dump
    def add_extra_data(self, data):
        obj = self.context.get('obj', None)
        for key, value in getattr(obj, 'extra_data', {}).items():
            data[key] = value
        return data

Regarding performance: Yes, if uninterpreted functions are used, Z3 will use the SMT solver and not the SAT solver, because the latter doesn't support UFs. The [internal encoding of to_ieee_bv](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/ast/fpa/fpa2bv_converter.cpp#L3063) only uses UFs for NaNs when `m_hi_fp_unspecified` (command line option `rewriter.hi_fp_unspecified`) is enabled (default is off). At this point I don't know whether we can get the same behavior by only using SMT-standard concepts, as that doesn't give us access to individual bits of the encoding (because they assume that (at least some) SMT solvers won't use an encoding based on bit-vectors where this is a no-op).

This is a control-flow recovery task - you want to run a CFG analysis (`project.analysis.CFGFast()`, you can tweak the arguments to limit the scope) and then the knowledge base will have function information available as `project.kb.functions`.

This does the trick:

```
def load(self, data):
        (self.steamIdChat,
         self.steamIdFriend,
         self.chatRoomType,
         self.steamIdOwner,
         self.steamIdClan,
         self.chatFlags,
         self.enterResponse,
         self.numMembers
         ) = struct.unpack_from("<QQIQQ?II", data)

        nullId = struct.calcsize("<QQIQQ?II") + data[struct.calcsize("<QQIQQ?II"):].index('\x00')
        self.chatRoomName = data[struct.calcsize("<QQIQQ?II"):nullId]

        for x in data[nullId+1:].split('\x08\x08')[:-1]:
                self.memberList.append(vdf.binary_loads(x+'\x08\x08'))

```

@WardLT Thanks to @nisse3000, the root of the structure corruption has been identified. It does not have to do with the code in this PR, but rather the pymatgen `Structure` and pickling. When oxidation states are present, the structures are corrupted after serialization/deserialization. 

```python
cscl = Structure(
    Lattice([[4.209, 0, 0], [0, 4.209, 0], [0, 0, 4.209]]),
    ["Cl1-", "Cs1+"], [[2.105, 2.105, 2.105], [0, 0, 0]],
    validate_proximity=False, to_unit_cell=False,
    coords_are_cartesian=True, site_properties=None)


pickle.dump(cscl, open('cscl.pickle', 'wb'))
print(pickle.load(open('cscl.pickle', 'rb')))
```
```
>>>
Full Formula (Cs1)
Reduced Formula: Cs
abc   :   4.209000   4.209000   4.209000
angles:  90.000000  90.000000  90.000000
Sites (2)
  #  SP           a         b         c
---  ----  --------  --------  --------
  0  Cs+   0.500119  0.500119  0.500119
  1  Cs+   0         0         0
```


Whereas the same structure with no oxidation states does not corrupt the structure:
```python
cscl_nooxi = Structure(
    Lattice([[4.209, 0, 0], [0, 4.209, 0], [0, 0, 4.209]]),
    ["Cl", "Cs"], [[2.105, 2.105, 2.105], [0, 0, 0]],
    validate_proximity=False, to_unit_cell=False,
    coords_are_cartesian=True, site_properties=None)

pickle.dump(cscl_nooxi, open('cscl_nooxi.pickle', 'wb'))
print(pickle.load(open('cscl_nooxi.pickle', 'rb')))
```
```
>>>
Full Formula (Cs1 Cl1)
Reduced Formula: CsCl
abc   :   4.209000   4.209000   4.209000
angles:  90.000000  90.000000  90.000000
Sites (2)
  #  SP           a         b         c
---  ----  --------  --------  --------
  0  Cl    0.500119  0.500119  0.500119
  1  Cs    0         0         0
```


The reason structures are corrupted in different ways during multiprocessing based on their list order is because (1) the corrupt pickled structures are chunked so their data can be processed, and (2) the order of the chunked data affects the exact way the structures are corrupted. 


I'll make an issue on pymatgen for this. However, since this issue does not originate in matminer, I think this PR can be merged.

    if 'treat_mw_power' in meas_data.columns:

causes problems, because the program then assumes that there is actual microwave data.

Since it appears there are easy fixes for submenus we should probably just patch it up as is in 0.8.X.

As for the 0.9.X branche, I have an alternate idea for submenus. I think it's possible to present subcommands (argparse sub-parsers) in a way that it looks like submenus. 

Commands using argparse can be decorated to indicate they want to present as a submenu. When one of these commands is processed, we can save it and flag that we're navigating down a particular command. Help and tab completion would then only present that command's subcommands. If there are more layers of subcommands, that would get pushed on to the command navigation stack and the next layer of subcommands would be available. When executing a command, we can automatically prepend the preceding layers command/subcommands before parsing the command.

Command history can be separately maintained when we go into this state just like with submenus today.
the shared attributes stuff from the current implementation wouldn't be needed because everything would be running in a single Cmd instance.

+1 for always checking `writable` and `ndims`. The more the interface resembles its Python counterpart (rather than the C API), it's natural to also expect the same Python-like behavior. Translating Python's runtime checks/exception into C++ undefined behavior would be pretty surprising in my opinion. The runtime checks aren't a big performance penalty for casual usage (where the checks are most important). Users looking to squeeze every last drop of performance will just take the data pointer once and the subsequent calculations will be much more expensive than the initial check.

As for `array::data()`, returning `void*` seems more natural to me since it makes no assumption about data type. Although, it may also make sense to have a `T* array::data<T>()` function which would do the cast internally and also check the data type is correct or throw.

Unfortunately, I'm also unable to reproduce locally and I have no idea why, but I understand the problem.

Google is building these objects and determining their attributes and methods dynamically based on metadata about the endpoint. If you search Google's Python libraries source code, you'll find no reference to `resourceRecordSets` and instead this is being returned by the Google server which the library interprets to create the object. `pylint` has no way of understanding this so it thinks the attribute doesn't exist. If you look at all the other attributes we use on this object, such as `changes` and `managedZones`, we also disable `no-member` there as well to make `pylint` happy.  Disabling this check in `pylint` for `resourceRecordSets` seems safe with this in mind and should fix the problem.

Thanks for making the ticket! 

As a quick solution - the courtesy close should be removed (i.e. the extra parameter provided in close()). 

In unidirectional channels (i.e. one way payments) - both Alice and Bob have the incentive to submit the transfer that pays them the most money. So an incremented counter isn't really necessary. Notably, if Alice is responsible for broadcasting the money she gets from Bob (i.e. B->A), and Bob is responsible for broadcasting the money he gets from Alice (i.e. A->B) - then technically the settlement channels requires both participants to sign and agree to it. This is going to be important when you start doing HTLC transactions. 

I think the nonce should still be preserved to prevent relay attacks for concurrently opened channels. I'll explain below: 

Right now (from what I understand) the nonce is greater than the channel's starting block height. 

When I say "channel" I mean a single unidirectional channel where Alice -> Bob. So just one-way payments. 

i.e. Alice and Bob open a channel at block height 100, the nonce is n=100, and then the channel closes at block height 200. 

If Alice and Bob open a new channel at block height 215, then any previous message (with the nonce n=100 are not valid. 

That works fine. But someday in the future - your smart contract might allow Alice and Bob to open two or more channels at the same time. 

For example, at block height 100 - Alice and Bob open two channels C1 and C2. The above nonce approach isn't going to work there as the counter for both channels is 100. 

So what I propose (this will need discussion) is that the nonce is the following:

nonce = H(block hash, channel id, deposit_tx_id, reset_counter).

The channel ID should be unique (perhaps it is based of the deposit transaction's id). This would then provide a nonce that is unique for each channel. So if the nonce for C1 is 1928d and the nonce for C2 is 17273e - the messages cannot be replayed in either channel. 

I mention a 'reset_counter' in the hash as well - because from what I understand currently - when either unidirectional channel exhausts its supply of bitcoins - then you need to call close() and re-open a new pair of unidirectional channels. Really this reset should be doable off-chain (like in Duplex Micropayment Channels by Decker) - so every time there is a reset the counter is incremented by 1. This `offchain reset' for Ethereum still needs a lot of thought, but it is worth mentioning as I suspect you will want to do something like this in the future.

It's already possible to run Ray inside of Docker, e.g., see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/docker/deploy/Dockerfile.

We're currently recommending that people run one Docker container per machine (with all of the relevant Ray processes inside of that docker container). The reason is that the worker processes need to be able to share memory with one each other to take advantage of the shared memory object store.

We don't have immediate plans to run individual worker processes in separate docker containers, although I can see why that would be useful. If you're interested in trying this out, I'm happy to help brainstorm how to do that.

As for running ray inside of a virtual env, I think this should already work. That is, if if you run `ray start` inside of a virtual env or a script that does `ray.init()` inside of a virtual env, then the worker processes should be started within the same virtual env. However, I haven't tested this extensively.

1) My worry about making GFS an `Implicit` is that it cannot be used in conjunction with an additional `TimeStepper` which steps the prognostics forward in time.  In general, I'm not happy assigning `Prognostic`s to an `Implicit` component. 

I would prefer GFS just error if it is supplied with a `Prognostic` which provides a tendency for a quantity which it does not step forward in time.

`self.prognostics` is set after GFS initialisation. The reason for this behaviour was to allow changing the physics used by GFS at any time. For instance, you might want to use some physics only before/after spin up, and this option allowed doing such a thing without having to restart the model.

No matter what strategy you use (wrt sequential tendency updates), the main issue remains -- eventually you will need to transform between physical and spectral spaces. If this is done at every timestep, we lose too much information and the dynamics does not evolve. Do you see a way around this?

2) Certainly all four tracers are not required. However, GFS keeps track of the first tracer, which is water vapour and is part of the dynamical equations. So, that tracer is a required input.

3) GFS only requires surface pressure and sigma levels, and it outputs 3D pressure field on mid and interface levels. for some strange reason, the calculation of pressure on interface levels in fortran flips the dimensions. Hence this code.

4) That is a hack to get GFS to work. There is a strange bug (that I never figured out the reason for) which would cause GFS to fail basic dynamics tests (DCMIP) if this line was commented out. I would suggest leaving it in and opening an issue to remind me to fix it if possible.

> Which begs the question of the fourth widget - Label without math support...

The case I keep forgetting. Math or no feels more to me like a vertical or horizontal slider question, i.e. appropriate to set on initialization/with a trait. If math were separate, then `Label` and `LabelNoMath` (not a good name) would be better for backwards compatibility.

Of course that minimizes consistency with HTML...

Thinking out loud here [and not seriously proposing markdown this week]:

### `Label` has a property like `render_style`

This would be like the `orientation` property for sliders, with value like `math` (default) and `plain` and perhaps even down the road `markdown`.

Advantage: flexible, backwards compatible, allows for future expansion without new classes.
Disadvantage: a user who doesn't know about `render_style` may not be likely to discover it, though perhaps that can be done in the documentation.

### Helper classes, analogous to `VBox` and `HBox` for containers

Like `Box` has some default orientation, `Label` would default to current behavior. `TextLabel` or `PlainLabel` does no latex interpreting, down the road `MarkdownLabel` does that, etc. Maybe even have a `MathLabel` which is the same as `Label`, just like `HBox` is essentially the same as `Box`.

Advantage: Explicit, easy enough to add additional example down the road.
Disadvantage: More verbose; potentially still hard to discover the right thing to do if you don't want math.

### Make `Label` not do math but `LabelMath` does math

Advantage: consistency with `HTML`, conceivably also tab-completion discoverable. Just as extensible as the previous option.
Disadvantage: Backwards compatibility, which is big. Plus, I like the magic of `Label($\mu$)` working just like I would expect, but that is not really a strong argument.

### Add documentation on how to avoid math in `Label`

It looks like (based on a couple of quick tests) using a backslash to escape the `$` keeps it interpreted as a literal. So `Label('$\mu$')` produces `µ` but `Label('\$\mu\$')` produces `$\mu$`.

The other ways of getting math, e.g. `\(\mu\)`, seem unlikely to happen unless a user intends for there to be math, so `$` may be the only character that needs special treatment for non-math users.

Advantage: Doc fixes are easier than code changes, preserves backwards-compatibility.
Disadvantage: The `$` requires special handling.
